title: Ethics and Professionalism in Teaching
content:
Ethics and Professionalism in Teaching (EDA 425)

Introduction

Teaching is a moral endeavor that extends far beyond the delivery of curriculum content; it requires a deep commitment to the welfare, dignity, and intellectual growth of students. Within the professional foundations of EDA 425: Teaching Practice II, pre-service teachers must internalize the ethical standards of the Nigerian teaching profession. This module examines the Teacher's Code of Conduct and the fundamental professional expectations that define the relationship between the educator, the students, the school administration, and the community. By understanding these boundaries, student-teachers build the trust and professional reputation necessary for a successful career.

Key Concepts

To operate professionally in a school setting, core ethical terms must be fully understood. Professional Integrity is the consistency of action, values, and methods, ensuring that a teacher’s behaviors inside and outside the classroom align with institutional and societal expectations. Duty of Care is the legal and moral obligation of the teacher to prioritize the safety, security, and well-being of all students under their supervision. Confidentiality entails the strict protection of sensitive information regarding student performance, personal histories, or behavioral issues, sharing these details only with authorized academic staff and parents.

Theoretical Framework

Professionalism is framed by the Virtue Ethics Model and the Code of Conduct enforced by the Teachers Registration Council of Nigeria (TRCN). Virtue ethics posits that effective teaching is driven by the internal character of the educator, including traits like patience, fairness, and wisdom. This is codified by the TRCN, which outlines the non-negotiable standards for professional behavior, classroom discipline, academic assessment, and the maintenance of a professional distance that protects both the teacher and the learner from potential impropriety.

Nigerian Context and Application

In the diverse, often high-stakes environments of Nigerian secondary schools, professionalism is tested daily. Student-teachers must navigate cultural nuances, local customs, and social hierarchies while maintaining a neutral, professional standard. Whether interacting with school principals, local community leaders, or parents from different socio-economic backgrounds, the teacher must remain a model of fairness. This involves avoiding favoritism, handling sensitive cultural discussions with respect, and ensuring that no student is disadvantaged by the teacher’s personal biases or communal affiliations.

Practical Classroom Implications

For the student-teacher during practicum, practicing ethics means arriving punctually, dressing in a professional and modest manner, and strictly avoiding unprofessional communication—such as venting about school frustrations on social media or entering into inappropriate personal relationships with students. Teachers must document all student disciplinary actions officially and maintain an objective, evidence-based approach to grading. Evaluation during Teaching Practice II specifically assesses a student-teacher's reliability, adherence to school policies, and ability to handle student information with mature, professional discretion.

Summary

Professionalism in the Nigerian educational system is defined by unwavering adherence to ethical conduct, the TRCN Code of Conduct, and the maintenance of a clear, protective professional boundary. By prioritizing student safety through a rigorous duty of care and practicing objective, fair assessment, student-teachers cultivate the trust required for long-term pedagogical success. Upholding these virtues not only protects the student-teacher's career but also elevates the social status of the teaching profession itself.

References

  • Teachers Registration Council of Nigeria (TRCN). (2018). Teachers’ Code of Conduct. Abuja: TRCN.
  • Opara, P. O. (2010). Professionalism in the Nigerian Teaching Service. Owerri: Education Press.
  • Campbell, E. (2003). The Ethical Teacher. Maidenhead: Open University Press.
  • Nwachukwu, C. C. (2015). Ethics and Practice of Education in Nigeria. Enugu: University Trust Publishers.

title: Lesson Delivery and Classroom Control
content:
Lesson Delivery and Classroom Control (EDA 425)

Introduction

Successful lesson delivery and effective classroom management are the twin pillars of instructional success. In EDA 425, Teaching Practice II provides the environment to refine the art of pedagogical performance and the science of behavioral influence. A teacher may have a flawless lesson plan, but without the ability to manage the room and keep students productively focused, the learning objectives will fail to take root. This module provides techniques for dynamic instruction and proactive classroom control, helping student-teachers transition from nervous trainees to confident, authoritative facilitators of learning.

Key Concepts

To master the classroom, core delivery and management terms must be internalized. Pedagogical Fluency is the ability to deliver instruction smoothly, utilizing clear pacing, effective voice projection, and natural movement that holds student interest. Proactive Management refers to the strategies—such as clearly defined expectations and organized lesson transitions—that prevent behavioral disruptions before they start. Reactive Management includes the specific, fair, and immediate techniques used to address disruptions that do occur, ensuring that instruction resumes with minimal delay.

Theoretical Framework

This module integrates the Direct Instruction Framework with Jacob Kounin’s Classroom Management Theory. Kounin’s research emphasizes the importance of With-it-ness (the teacher’s awareness of all classroom activity) and Overlapping (the ability to handle multiple tasks, like grading and monitoring, simultaneously). Combined with structured direct instruction—where the teacher clearly models the task, provides guided practice, and facilitates independent application—these frameworks allow the teacher to minimize idle time and maximize the students' engagement in the lesson objectives.

Nigerian Context and Application

Classroom control in many Nigerian schools is complicated by high student-to-teacher ratios. In crowded settings, a teacher cannot simply talk to the board; they must be physically mobile, circulating through the rows to keep all students engaged. This 'circulating instruction' strategy is essential for managing behavior in busy classrooms. By moving throughout the room, the teacher naturally breaks up side conversations and keeps distant students attentive, ensuring the learning experience is inclusive rather than limited to the front rows.

Practical Classroom Implications

For the student-teacher, success begins with a structured lesson opening that grabs attention and states clear, measurable goals. Teachers must utilize varied Instructional Hooks—such as a relevant question, a compelling quote, or a brief demonstration—to spark curiosity. During the lesson, they must maintain a professional and steady voice, avoiding the urge to shout over noise, which only increases stress. Instead, they should use proximity (standing near disruptive students), eye contact, and clear, non-verbal cues to regain control. Evaluation metrics for TP II focus on the teacher's ability to maintain high engagement levels and effectively handle typical classroom management challenges with calm, consistent authority.

Summary

Effective instruction relies on the synergy of dynamic lesson delivery and structured classroom management, underpinned by Kounin’s 'with-it-ness' and direct instruction techniques. By prioritizing proactive strategies like physical circulation and clear lesson transitions, student-teachers can manage even the most densely populated classrooms. Developing a calm, authoritative presence ensures that the learning environment remains focused, orderly, and highly productive throughout the duration of the Teaching Practice period.

References

  • Kounin, J. S. (1970). Discipline and Group Management in Classrooms. New York: Holt, Rinehart and Winston.
  • Emmer, E. T., & Evertson, C. M. (2017). Classroom Management for Middle and High School Teachers. Boston: Pearson.
  • Egunjobi, A. O. (2012). Pedagogical Skills and Classroom Management in Nigerian Schools. Ibadan: Spectrum Books.

title: Instructional Media and Resource Management
content:
Instructional Media and Resource Management (EDA 425)

Introduction

Instructional media consists of the physical or digital tools—ranging from chalkboards and visual charts to educational apps and multimedia projectors—used to bridge the gap between abstract concepts and student understanding. In the practical module of EDA 425, effectively utilizing instructional media is a hallmark of an advanced teacher. Relying solely on textbooks is rarely sufficient to engage the diverse learning needs of modern secondary students. This module teaches student-teachers how to source, adapt, and deploy a variety of low-cost and high-tech resources to make their classroom instruction vivid, memorable, and highly impactful.

Key Concepts

To implement media effectively, core resource terms must be clearly understood. Instructional Media includes any device, model, or visual aid designed to facilitate learning by clarifying complex information or stimulating student sensory engagement. Improvisation is the pedagogical skill of creating highly effective learning tools out of readily available, locally sourced materials—essential when formal high-tech resources are unavailable. Multimedia Integration is the deliberate combination of text, images, and audio to target multiple learning styles (auditory, visual, and kinesthetic) simultaneously, creating a more robust cognitive map for the students.

Theoretical Framework

This module is grounded in Edgar Dale’s Cone of Experience, which posits that learning is deepest when students move beyond reading and hearing toward active, concrete experiences like demonstrations, role-plays, and purposeful sensory involvement. Media is not a decoration; it is a catalyst. By carefully choosing media that moves students up the 'cone'—from passive observation to active engagement—the teacher accelerates comprehension and increases the long-term retention of lesson concepts.

Nigerian Context and Application

In many Nigerian secondary schools, the most effective instructional media is not the most expensive. Given potential constraints in reliable electricity or internet connectivity, student-teachers must become masters of Instructional Improvisation. Creating local models, using physical maps, utilizing the local environment for science experiments, or preparing well-designed, hand-drawn posters can often be more effective than a high-tech slide presentation. The key is relevance: the teacher must adapt their resources to the local environment and the students’ cultural context to ensure the media feels meaningful rather than foreign or distracting.

Practical Classroom Implications

For the student-teacher, effective media use requires meticulous planning. Every chart, model, or digital aid must be prepared before the lesson begins, tested for clarity, and strategically introduced at the moment it provides the greatest impact. Teachers must avoid 'media overload,' where the tool distracts from the lesson content rather than supporting it. Evaluation of this skill focuses on the relevance of the chosen media to the specific learning objectives and the teacher’s ease and confidence in deploying these tools within a live, fast-paced classroom setting.

Summary

Mastering instructional media relies on understanding the cognitive power of visual and kinesthetic tools, as mapped out by Dale's Cone of Experience. By prioritizing effective, locally improvised resources alongside high-tech tools, student-teachers can transform abstract lessons into concrete, engaging experiences. Demonstrating versatility and creativity in resource management shows a high level of pedagogical maturity and significantly boosts overall student achievement levels during the teaching practicum.

References

  • Dale, E. (1969). Audio-Visual Methods in Teaching. New York: Dryden Press.
  • Osuji, S. N. (2010). Instructional Media and Materials in Nigerian Schools. Owerri: African Heritage Press.
  • Ibe-Bassey, G. S. (2000). Instructional Communication and Media in Education. Uyo: Dorand Publishers.

title: Student Assessment and Record Keeping
content:
Student Assessment and Record Keeping (EDA 425)

Introduction

Assessment is the diagnostic process through which a teacher evaluates student progress, identifies learning gaps, and refines their instructional approach. Record keeping is the administrative bedrock that ensures these assessments are valid, transparent, and legally defensible. In EDA 425, managing these responsibilities with accuracy and fairness is a critical requirement for a successful teaching practicum. This module focuses on the development of valid, reliable assessments and the systematic maintenance of academic records, showing student-teachers how to handle data responsibly and communicate student progress professionally.

Key Concepts

To handle assessment administration, core terms must be practiced. Formative Assessment is the ongoing, low-stakes evaluative process (e.g., quizzes, class discussions, exit tickets) used to gather 'real-time' feedback and adjust teaching while learning is happening. Summative Assessment is the high-stakes evaluation—such as mid-term exams or final projects—used to measure student mastery at the end of a unit. Grade Book Integrity refers to the organized, accurate, and consistent entry of all assignment, exam, and behavioral records, ensuring they are always available for review by students, parents, and administrators.

Theoretical Framework

This module aligns with the Balanced Assessment Framework, which asserts that quality instruction requires a healthy mix of different evaluative tools to capture the full spectrum of student ability. By combining objective multiple-choice items with subjective tasks like essay writing, oral presentations, and practical projects, the teacher accounts for diverse intelligence types and learning styles. The framework emphasizes validity (measuring what is intended) and reliability (ensuring assessment results are consistent across different observers).

Nigerian Context and Application

In the Nigerian educational landscape, maintaining clear and transparent assessment records is vital for accountability. Student-teachers will regularly engage with Continuous Assessment (CA) scores, which hold significant weight in final student rankings. Every assignment, class test, and homework submission must be recorded systematically in a permanent record book, with clear documentation of the criteria used for grading. This transparency is crucial for maintaining a professional relationship with parents and school management, as it allows for clear, evidence-based conversations regarding a student’s academic standing.

Practical Classroom Implications

For the student-teacher, assessment begins with creating a clear grading rubric for every major task. Students must know before they start what 'success' looks like. During class, the teacher should employ frequent, low-stress formative checks—such as observing student work, walking the room, and using quick Q&A sessions—to ensure comprehension before moving on to new material. Every assessment, no matter how small, must be graded fairly and returned to the student promptly, as delayed feedback loses its developmental value.

Summary

Successful student assessment relies on balancing formative and summative evaluative tools within a robust, transparent framework. By maintaining organized and accurate grade records, student-teachers demonstrate the professional accountability required by school boards and state examination bodies. Adhering to these standards ensures that the student-teacher provides fair, actionable feedback that empowers students to improve their academic performance throughout the entire school term.

References

  • Black, P., & Wiliam, D. (1998). Inside the Black Box: Raising Standards Through Classroom Assessment. Phi Delta Kappan, 80(2), 139-148.
  • Obi, T. C. (2012). Principles and Practice of Educational Measurement and Evaluation. Enugu: University Trust Publishers.

title: Reflective Journaling and Peer Observation
content:
Reflective Journaling and Peer Observation (EDA 425)

Introduction

Teaching is an iterative process that requires constant self-correction, analysis, and refinement. Reflective practice—the ability to look back on one’s own classroom performance, evaluate what happened, and determine how to improve—is the hallmark of a master educator. In EDA 425, the use of reflective journals and the engagement in peer observation serve as the primary mechanisms for this professional growth. This module teaches student-teachers how to document their daily teaching experiences and open their classrooms to constructive criticism, transforming every lesson into a laboratory for improvement.

Key Concepts

To cultivate a reflective mindset, core developmental terms must be practiced. Reflective Journaling is the disciplined practice of documenting specific classroom episodes—what went well, what struggled, and what surprised the teacher—to analyze pedagogical patterns. Peer Observation is the collaborative act of allowing another student-teacher or a mentor to observe your instruction and provide focused, evidence-based feedback. Metacognitive Analysis is the high-level evaluation where the teacher examines not just the lesson itself, but their own thinking and planning process that led to that specific lesson outcome.

Theoretical Framework

This module follows Donald Schön’s 'Reflective Practitioner' Framework, which distinguishes between 'reflection-in-action' (the split-second adjustments a teacher makes while the lesson is running) and 'reflection-on-action' (the deeper, post-lesson analysis). By combining these with Vygotsky’s principles of social learning—where feedback from peers serves as a vital 'scaffold' for professional development—the teacher creates a closed-loop system of continuous improvement that moves far beyond basic training.

Summary

Professional growth relies on the rigorous application of reflective journaling and the active, collaborative process of peer observation, rooted in Schön’s reflective practitioner frameworks. By capturing lessons learned in daily logs and inviting constructive feedback from colleagues, student-teachers accelerate their pedagogical development. Embracing this cycle of reflection transforms the daunting experience of the teaching practicum into a clear, structured path toward becoming a highly capable and adaptive professional educator.

References

  • Schön, D. A. (1983). The Reflective Practitioner: How Professionals Think in Action. New York: Basic Books.
  • Pollard, A. (2014). Reflective Teaching in Schools (4th ed.). London: Bloomsbury Academic.

title: Community and Staff Relationships
content:
Community and Staff Relationships (EDA 425)

Introduction

No teacher works in isolation; a school is a dense web of professional relationships involving colleagues, school administrators, support staff, and the wider local community. In EDA 425, building positive community and staff relationships is a critical component of the Teaching Practice II experience. A student-teacher who treats their colleagues with respect and effectively engages with the local community is a teacher who will always be supported. This module examines the essential soft skills—communication, collaboration, and cultural intelligence—required to navigate school life as a valued and integrated member of the staff.

Key Concepts

To cultivate a supportive school culture, core relational terms must be understood. Collaborative Professionalism is the proactive effort to work with other staff members, sharing resources, teaching methods, and responsibilities to improve the overall quality of school life. Community Engagement is the ability to connect the curriculum to the local reality, respecting community leaders, and establishing a professional presence that reflects positively on the institution.

Practical Classroom Implications

For the student-teacher, success begins with being a helpful, reliable, and active member of the staff room. Teachers should seek out opportunities to collaborate with colleagues, share their own digital teaching skills, or assist in common school tasks without being asked. When communication issues arise, the student-teacher should always choose a professional, private, and calm approach to resolution, avoiding school gossip or factional disputes. Evaluation during Teaching Practice II specifically assesses the student-teacher's integration into the school staff, their respect for school administrative hierarchy, and their professional rapport with both colleagues and community members.

Summary

Cultivating positive relationships with school staff and the local community is essential for creating a stable, supportive teaching environment, heavily supported by the principles of systems theory in education. By actively contributing to staff collaboration, respecting school hierarchies, and engaging respectfully with community stakeholders, student-teachers build a strong, reliable support network. Developing these professional interpersonal skills ensures that the teacher is not just an individual practitioner, but a well-integrated, valued member of the entire school ecosystem.

References

  • Hargreaves, A., & O'Connor, M. T. (2018). Collaborative Professionalism. Thousand Oaks: Corwin Press.
  • Ukeje, B. O. (2012). School Administration in Nigeria. Enugu: University Trust Publishers.

title: Post-TP Evaluation and Portfolio Defense
content:
Post-TP Evaluation and Portfolio Defense (EDA 425)

Introduction

The final milestone of EDA 425: Teaching Practice II is the comprehensive portfolio defense and final professional evaluation. This is not just a formality; it is the official synthesis of everything the student-teacher has learned, implemented, and reflected upon during their time in the field. The portfolio serves as the definitive record of professional growth, while the defense allows the student-teacher to justify their teaching decisions to an academic panel. This module outlines the steps for crafting a high-impact portfolio and preparing for the final defense, ensuring that the Teaching Practice experience concludes on a professional, high-achieving note.

Key Concepts

To finalize the practicum successfully, core exit competencies must be understood. The Teaching Portfolio is a curated collection of a teacher’s best work—including lesson plans, assessment rubrics, student work samples, reflective journals, and video evidence—that serves as a comprehensive record of the student-teacher's classroom performance. Portfolio Defense is the final oral presentation where the teacher maps their classroom experiences against the theoretical standards learned in the university, proving they are ready to become a professional, autonomous classroom leader.

Summary

Completing the post-TP evaluation successfully relies on compiling a structured, evidence-based portfolio that bridges the gap between university theory and classroom practice. By showcasing clear evidence of student progress and demonstrating a deep, reflective understanding of their own growth, student-teachers can confidently defend their professional readiness. A meticulously prepared portfolio and a strong final presentation serve as the crowning achievement of the undergraduate education journey, marking the transition from a trainee to a fully qualified professional educator.

References

  • Danielson, C. (2013). The Framework for Teaching Evaluation Instrument. Princeton: The Danielson Group.
  • Campbell, D. M., et al. (2013). How to Develop a Professional Portfolio. Boston: Pearson.
